programs.name: TennCare Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
Tennessee State Health Insurance Assistance Program (TN SHIP)
Tennessee Meals on Wheels
programs.tagline: If your parent is 60+ and homebound, they may qualify for free daily meals delivered to their door.

localEntities.name: Local Area Agencies on Aging and Disability (AAAD)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggles to prepare nutritious meals due to illness or disability, Tennessee Meals on Wheels delivers a hot, balanced meal to their door Monday through Friday. Each meal provides one-third of their daily nutritional needs, plus a friendly visit and safety check from volunteers.

Unlike many senior programs, Tennessee Meals on Wheels has no income or asset limits — eligibility is based entirely on age and need for assistance. However, your parent must be assessed by their local Area Agency on Aging to qualify for home delivery. The program operates in all 95 Tennessee counties, but each region has different providers and contact numbers.

summary: Homebound due to illness or disability
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound due to illness, incapacitation, disability, or inability to prepare nutritious meals. Requires assessment by local Area Agency on Aging.
otherRequirements: Must pass intake assessment by local Area Agency on Aging

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide number to connect with your local Area Agency on Aging for an assessment — processing time varies by county.

steps.title: Call your local Area Agency on Aging
steps.description: Use the statewide number 1-866-836-6678 to be connected to your county's office, or find your local number in the directory below.

steps.title: Complete intake assessment
steps.description: A case manager will assess your parent's need for meal delivery based on their ability to prepare food and leave home safely.

steps.title: Schedule meal delivery
steps.description: If approved, your local provider will schedule Monday-Friday meal delivery and assign volunteer drivers.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by county — Knox County starts delivery in 2-3 business days, other counties require assessment with unspecified timeline
applicationGuide.tip: Some counties have faster processing than others — Knox County's CAC Mobile Meals starts delivery within 2-3 business days.

contentSections.text: Tennessee Meals on Wheels is not one centralized program — each county has different providers and slight variations in eligibility. You must contact your local Area Agency on Aging for exact requirements and availability.
Congregate meals at senior centers are available to anyone 60+ without the homebound requirement, and spouses under 60 can participate too. Ask about both options when you call.

faqs.question: Does my parent have to be completely homebound to qualify?
faqs.answer: No, but they must be homebound due to illness, disability, or inability to safely prepare nutritious meals. The local Area Agency on Aging will assess their specific situation during intake.

faqs.question: What if my parent can sometimes leave the house but shouldn't cook?
faqs.answer: They may still qualify for home delivery if cooking is unsafe due to their condition. If not, they can attend congregate meals at local senior centers, which don't require the homebound assessment.

faqs.question: Are the meals really free, or is there a cost?
faqs.answer: Most providers offer meals by suggested donation, meaning they're free but donations are welcomed. Some areas like Knox County have paid options. Confirm the cost structure when you call your local office.

faqs.question: Can my dad get meals if he's under 60 but disabled?
faqs.answer: Generally no for home delivery — that's typically 60+ only. However, some regions allow people 18-59 with mental or physical challenges to receive congregate meals, and disabled people under 60 can eat at senior center sites if they live in senior high-rise housing.

faqs.question: What happens if my parent goes to the hospital or travels?
faqs.answer: Contact your local provider to pause and resume delivery. Since each county has different providers, the policies for temporary stops will vary.

faqs.question: How do I find out which meals program serves my parent's address?
faqs.answer: Call the statewide number 1-866-836-6678 and they'll connect you to the right local Area Agency on Aging based on your parent's address.

programs.name: National Family Caregiver Support Program (NFCSP)
programs.tagline: If you're 55+ caring for an aging parent or disabled family member, get up to 6 hours of weekly respite care plus caregiver training and support groups.
programs.intro: If you're 55 or older and caring for an aging parent or disabled family member, NFCSP provides respite care, training, and support services to help you maintain your caregiving role. The program offers up to 6 hours per week of in-home respite care, plus access to caregiver support groups, training sessions, and services like adult daycare or homemaker assistance. Your parent must be 60+ or have Alzheimer's/dementia, or you must be caring for a disabled adult relative (18-59) or grandchild under 18. The person you're caring for must need help with at least 2 daily activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management, or require supervision due to cognitive impairment. Services are free if your parent's income is below 185% of federal poverty guidelines ($2,721/month for one person in 2026), though you may be asked to contribute based on costs if income is higher.

summary: Caring for parent 60+ or disabled relative
Care recipient needs help with 2+ daily activities
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Care recipient must be unable to perform at least 2 Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, transferring, continence) without substantial human assistance, OR have cognitive impairment requiring supervision to prevent harm.
otherRequirements: Must be family member or informal caregiver, not paid professional
Care recipient must live in the community (not nursing home)
Care recipient income at/above 185% Federal Poverty Guidelines ($2,721/month in 2026) may require cost contributions

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide intake line at 1-866-836-6678 to complete eligibility screening over the phone.

steps.title: Call intake line
steps.description: Call 1-866-836-6678 and speak with an Intake Specialist who will ask questions to determine your eligibility.

steps.title: Complete phone screening
steps.description: Answer questions about your caregiving situation and your parent's needs. The specialist will score your responses.

steps.title: Wait for case manager contact
steps.description: If your score qualifies and a slot is available, case management staff will contact you to schedule an assessment.

steps.title: Schedule home visit
steps.description: A case manager will visit your home to complete a full assessment of needs and available services.

steps.title: Receive service plan
steps.description: The case manager will create a service plan and connect you with available local providers.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Timeline varies by region and slot availability
applicationGuide.waitlist: Services may not be immediately available. Priority given to caregivers with greatest economic and social needs.
applicationGuide.tip: Have information ready about your parent's specific care needs and daily limitations when you call.

contentSections.text: Even if you qualify, services may not be available immediately. Priority goes to caregivers with the greatest economic and social needs, and availability varies significantly by region.
You are the client in this program, even though services are provided to your parent. The program is designed to support you as the caregiver, not to directly serve your parent.

faqs.question: What if I'm under 55 but caring for my disabled adult child?
faqs.answer: You qualify as a parent caring for an adult child (18-59) with disabilities, regardless of your age. The 55+ age requirement only applies to other family relationships like grandparents, siblings, or other relatives.

faqs.question: Can I get help if Mom lives in assisted living?
faqs.answer: No. Your parent must live in the community (their own home, your home, or similar community setting). Residents of nursing homes or institutional care facilities are not eligible.

faqs.question: What if Mom's income is too high for free services?
faqs.answer: If your parent's income exceeds 185% of federal poverty guidelines ($2,721/month in 2026), you may be asked to contribute toward the cost of services, but you won't be denied help entirely. The contribution amount is based on actual service costs.

faqs.question: Is the 6 hours of respite care guaranteed?
faqs.answer: The up to 6 hours per week of in-home respite is the most consistently available service across Tennessee. Other services like support groups, training, or adult daycare depend on what's available in your specific area.

faqs.question: What if Mom only has mild memory problems but doesn't need help with bathing or dressing?
faqs.answer: If she has cognitive impairment that requires substantial supervision to prevent harm to herself or others, she may still qualify even without physical care needs. The key is whether she needs supervision due to memory or thinking problems.

faqs.question: How long can I receive services?
faqs.answer: There's no set time limit, but services are reassessed periodically. As long as you continue to meet eligibility requirements and services remain available in your area, you can continue receiving support.

programs.name: Tennessee Senior Law Alliance (TSLA) / Tennessee Senior Legal Helpline
programs.tagline: Free legal help for Tennessee seniors 60+ with wills, benefits disputes, housing issues, and elder abuse — no income limits.

localEntities.name: Legal Aid Society of Middle TN and The Cumberlands
Tennessee Alliance for Legal Services (statewide helpline)

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and living in Tennessee, they can get free legal help regardless of their income or savings. The Tennessee Senior Legal Helpline provides everything from drafting wills and powers of attorney to fighting wrongfully denied benefits and addressing elder abuse cases.

Unlike most legal aid programs, there's no income test — if your parent is 60+ and a Tennessee resident, they qualify. Services include full legal representation, estate planning documents, help with Medicare or Social Security disputes, housing problems, and consumer fraud cases. The program reaches all 95 Tennessee counties through four regional providers, with attorneys who specialize in senior legal issues.

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide helpline at 1-844-HELP4TN — attorneys are available to help immediately.

steps.title: Call the statewide helpline
steps.description: Dial 1-844-HELP4TN (1-844-435-7486). This connects you to Tennessee Alliance for Legal Services, which coordinates help across all regions.

steps.title: Describe your parent's legal issue
steps.description: Explain whether you need estate planning, benefits help, housing assistance, or other legal services. The intake specialist will match you with the right regional provider.

steps.title: Get connected to local attorney
steps.description: You'll be transferred or given direct contact information for the legal aid provider covering your parent's county. In East Tennessee, your parent must personally request help before in-person appointments are scheduled.

applicationGuide.tip: Use the statewide helpline (844-HELP4TN) rather than trying to figure out which regional provider serves your area — they'll route you correctly.

offices.name: Tennessee Alliance for Legal Services (Statewide Helpline)
Legal Aid Society of Middle TN and The Cumberlands
Legal Aid of East Tennessee - Legal Assistance for the Elderly

contentSections.text: This program has no income or asset limits — unusual for legal aid. Your parent qualifies based only on age (60+) and Tennessee residency, making it accessible even for middle-income seniors.

faqs.question: What if my parent has significant savings or assets?
faqs.answer: They still qualify. Unlike most legal aid programs, Tennessee's Senior Legal Helpline has no income or asset limits. As long as your parent is 60+ and lives in Tennessee, they can get free legal help regardless of their financial situation.

faqs.question: Can they help if Medicare wrongfully denied my parent's claim?
faqs.answer: Yes. The program specifically handles cases where benefits have been wrongfully reduced or denied, including Medicare, Medicaid, and Social Security disputes. They provide full legal representation, not just advice.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to get a will and power of attorney drafted?
faqs.answer: Nothing. All services are completely free, including estate planning documents like wills, advance directives, and powers of attorney. There are no fees or co-pays.

faqs.question: What if my parent lives in a rural Tennessee county?
faqs.answer: They're covered. The program reaches all 95 Tennessee counties through four regional providers. Call the statewide helpline at 1-844-HELP4TN and they'll connect you to the provider serving your parent's area.

faqs.question: Can they help with elder abuse or financial exploitation?
faqs.answer: Yes, abuse and exploitation cases are specifically listed as covered services. The attorneys can help with both legal protection and recovery of stolen assets.

faqs.question: Do I need to call for my parent or can they call directly?
faqs.answer: Either way works. However, in East Tennessee specifically, your parent must personally contact the office and request help before in-person appointments will be scheduled, even if you make the initial call.

programs.name: Tennessee Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocacy when your parent faces problems in a nursing home or assisted living facility.

localEntities.name: Districts include First TN (1), East TN (2), Southeast TN (3), Upper Cumberland (4), and others; full list at state site[8]

programs.intro:
If your parent lives in a Tennessee nursing home, assisted living facility, or adult care home and faces problems with their care, the Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program provides free advocacy to investigate and resolve complaints. Because ombudsmen work independently from facilities, they can address issues like poor care quality, billing disputes, abuse concerns, or wrongful discharge threats without conflicts of interest.

The program serves residents in approximately 700 facilities across 95 counties through 9 district offices. Your parent doesn't need to meet any income or asset requirements — services are available to all facility residents and their families. You can contact the ombudsman on your parent's behalf to file complaints or get information about resident rights.

summary: Resident of certified long-term care facility
otherRequirements: Must be a resident of a certified long-term care facility (nursing homes, assisted living facilities, homes for the aged, adult care homes), or family member acting on behalf of such a resident

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide toll-free number and you'll be connected to your district ombudsman immediately.

steps.title: Call the statewide number
steps.description: Dial 877-236-0013 and you'll be automatically routed to the district ombudsman who covers your parent's facility.

steps.title: Explain the situation
steps.description: Describe the problem or concern. No forms or documentation required — the ombudsman will investigate directly.

steps.title: Work with your ombudsman
steps.description: Your district ombudsman will investigate the complaint and work to resolve it with the facility, keeping you informed throughout the process.

applicationGuide.tip: You can call even for questions about your parent's rights — you don't need to wait until there's a serious problem.

contentSections.text: The ombudsman provides advocacy and complaint resolution, not direct healthcare or financial assistance. They investigate problems and work with facilities to resolve them, but don't provide medical care or financial aid.

faqs.question: Can I call the ombudsman if my parent is afraid to complain directly?
faqs.answer: Yes. Family members can contact the ombudsman on behalf of their parent. The ombudsman will handle the complaint professionally and work to protect your parent from any retaliation.

faqs.question: What kinds of problems can the ombudsman help with?
faqs.answer: The ombudsman investigates care quality issues, billing disputes, concerns about abuse or neglect, wrongful discharge threats, and violations of resident rights like privacy or freedom of movement. They also provide information about admission processes and long-term care options.

faqs.question: Will calling the ombudsman make the facility angry at my parent?
faqs.answer: Ombudsmen are trained to handle complaints professionally and facilities are prohibited from retaliating against residents who file complaints. The ombudsman works to resolve issues while protecting your parent's relationship with the facility.

faqs.question: How quickly will someone respond to my call?
faqs.answer: When you call the statewide number, you're immediately routed to your district ombudsman who covers your parent's county. Response times for investigating specific complaints vary based on the severity and complexity of the issue.

faqs.question: Does this cost anything?
faqs.answer: No. All ombudsman services are completely free. There are no income limits, fees, or eligibility requirements beyond your parent being a resident of a certified long-term care facility in Tennessee.

faqs.question: What if my parent's facility is in a different county than where I live?
faqs.answer: The district assignment is based on where your parent's facility is located, not where you live. The statewide number (877-236-0013) will automatically connect you to the right district ombudsman for your parent's facility.

programs.name: Senior Advocate Program
programs.tagline: If your parent falls through the cracks of other programs, dedicated staff help navigate red tape for food, housing, and safety needs.

localEntities.name: McMinn Senior Activity Center, Athens, TN
Southeast TN Area Agency on Aging and Disability
Other AAADs via statewide toll-free numbers

programs.intro:
If your parent is 60+ and struggling with food insecurity, home repairs, or safety issues but doesn't qualify for other programs, Tennessee's Senior Advocate Program provides dedicated staff to navigate red tape and fill gaps. Because this targets economically disadvantaged seniors who fall between the cracks of other assistance, advocates help access resources that cover up to 25% of financial needs not met elsewhere.

This isn't a statewide program — only three dedicated programs exist in Tennessee, with regional coverage through Area Agencies on Aging. East Tennessee has the most comprehensive services through ETHRA, which also provides legal assistance for seniors facing urgent situations that could cause harm if not addressed quickly.

otherRequirements: Typically don't qualify for other assistance programs
For legal help: must personally contact program office

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional office directly — priority cases get immediate attention, others are first-come, first-served.

steps.title: Contact your regional office
steps.description: Call the Area Agency on Aging serving your parent's county. For East Tennessee legal assistance, call (865) 691-2551.

steps.title: Explain your parent's situation
steps.description: Describe what gaps in services your parent faces — food insecurity, safety issues, home repairs, or other needs not covered by other programs.

steps.title: Get matched with an advocate
steps.description: If capacity allows, staff will work directly with your parent to navigate resources and coordinate with partners like housing authority, VA, and Social Security.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Priority cases given immediate attention; others first-come, first-served as capacity allows
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, when caseload reaches maximum capacity — option to join waiting list or get referrals to other services
applicationGuide.tip: Emphasize any urgent safety or health risks when you call — these cases get priority over routine requests.

offices.name: ETHRA East Tennessee Legal Assistance
offices.address: 9111 Cross Park Drive Ste. D-100, Knoxville, TN 37923

contentSections.text: Only 3 dedicated Senior Advocate programs exist in Tennessee. If your region doesn't have one, you may face waitlists or need referrals to other services.
This program specifically targets seniors who don't qualify elsewhere — don't assume your parent won't qualify just because other programs turned them down.

faqs.question: What if Mom doesn't qualify for SNAP or Medicaid but still needs help?
faqs.answer: This program specifically targets seniors who fall through the cracks of other assistance. Staff help navigate resources for food insecurity, home repairs, and safety needs even when traditional programs say no.

faqs.question: How much financial help can the program actually provide?
faqs.answer: The program aims to fill up to 25% of financial needs not covered by other programs, but this varies by region and capacity. Much of the help comes through advocacy and connecting to resources rather than direct payments.

faqs.question: What counts as a 'priority case' for immediate attention?
faqs.answer: Cases where delay could risk harm or death get priority — things like unsafe housing conditions, medication access issues, or food insecurity leading to health problems.

faqs.question: Can Dad get legal help even if he can't afford a lawyer?
faqs.answer: Yes, through ETHRA in East Tennessee. Legal assistance is available for seniors 60+ who personally contact the program office, with priority for urgent cases and others served as capacity allows.

faqs.question: What if our county doesn't have a Senior Advocate Program?
faqs.answer: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging and Disability. While only 3 dedicated programs exist statewide, all 9 regional AAADs may offer similar advocacy services or referrals.

faqs.question: Does Mom need to prove she's low-income to qualify?
faqs.answer: There are no specific statewide income limits identified. Local programs target economically disadvantaged seniors, but eligibility focuses more on service gaps than strict financial cutoffs.

programs.name: Tennessee State Health Insurance Assistance Program (TN SHIP) and Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP)
programs.tagline: Free one-on-one help navigating Medicare options, appeals, and fraud protection for your parent.

localEntities.name: 9 Area Agencies on Aging and Disability (AAADs) statewide; e.g., Aging Commission of the Mid-South, Upper Cumberland AAAD

programs.intro:
If your parent is overwhelmed by Medicare choices or suspects billing fraud, Tennessee's free counseling service can help. SHIP provides unbiased, one-on-one guidance on Medicare enrollment, plan comparisons, and appeals — no income limits, no sales pressure. The Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP) component helps protect your parent from Medicare fraud and teaches them to spot suspicious billing.

Because SHIP counselors don't sell insurance, they can objectively compare all Medicare Advantage and Part D plans available in your parent's area. They also help with applications for programs like Extra Help (prescription drug cost assistance) and Medicare Savings Programs that can reduce premiums and out-of-pocket costs.

The service is available statewide through 9 regional offices, with trained volunteers and staff who understand Medicare's complexity. During Open Enrollment (October 15 – December 7), demand is highest, but year-round counseling is available for urgent issues.

otherRequirements: Medicare beneficiary or helping someone with Medicare

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide hotline and get connected to your local counselor immediately.

steps.title: Call the statewide number
steps.description: Dial 1-877-801-0044. This routes you to your local Area Agency on Aging and Disability based on your county.

steps.title: Describe your Medicare question
steps.description: Whether it's plan comparison, enrollment help, appeals, or suspected fraud, explain what you need help with.

steps.title: Schedule counseling
steps.description: Get immediate phone guidance or schedule an in-person appointment. Bring your parent's Medicare card and current plan information if available.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate phone counseling; plan comparisons may take days during Open Enrollment
applicationGuide.tip: Call early in Open Enrollment season (starts October 15) to avoid delays in plan comparisons.

categories.name: Medicare Information
items: Medicare card (red, white, and blue card with Medicare number)
Current Medicare Advantage or Part D plan details
List of current medications and dosages

categories.name: Financial (for Extra Help applications only)
items: Social Security statement or pension information

offices.name: Statewide Hotline
offices.address: Routes to your local Area Agency on Aging and Disability

offices.name: Upper Cumberland Region
offices.address: Covers 14 counties in upper middle Tennessee

contentSections.text: If your parent becomes Medicare-eligible, they must disenroll from any ACA Marketplace plan to avoid penalties. SHIP can help coordinate this transition.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help if my parent already has Medicare Advantage?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors can review your parent's current plan, compare it to other options during Open Enrollment, and help with appeals if claims are denied or coverage changes unexpectedly.

faqs.question: What if my parent thinks their Medicare is being billed fraudulently?
faqs.answer: The Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP) component specifically handles fraud prevention and reporting. Call 1-866-836-7677 to report suspicious billing or get education on protecting your parent from Medicare scams.

faqs.question: Is this just for low-income seniors?
faqs.answer: No income limits apply for SHIP counseling services. However, SHIP can also help determine if your parent qualifies for income-based programs like Extra Help (prescription assistance) or Medicare Savings Programs.

faqs.question: Will they try to sell us insurance?
faqs.answer: No. SHIP counselors are specifically prohibited from promoting any particular insurance company or plan. They provide unbiased comparisons of all available options in your parent's area.

faqs.question: Can I get help outside of Open Enrollment season?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP provides year-round counseling for Medicare questions, appeals, fraud concerns, and qualifying life events that allow plan changes. Only routine plan switches are limited to Open Enrollment (October 15 – December 7).

faqs.question: What if I can't reach anyone during busy periods?
faqs.answer: During peak Open Enrollment season, try calling early morning or late afternoon. For immediate Medicare questions that can't wait, you can also call Medicare directly at 1-800-MEDICARE (1-800-633-4227).

stateOverview.intro: Tennessee offers 6 key programs to help you support your aging parent — 3 provide direct financial benefits or services, and 3 offer free assistance navigating the system. Benefits range from free daily meal delivery for homebound seniors to 6 hours of weekly respite care for family caregivers. Free resources include legal help with no income limits, Medicare guidance, and advocacy when nursing homes create problems. Start with the programs that address your biggest immediate need — whether that's daily meals, caregiver burnout, or legal issues your parent is facing.

startHere.name: Tennessee Meals on Wheels
startHere.why: If your parent struggles with grocery shopping or cooking, daily meal delivery provides immediate nutrition support.

startHere.name: National Family Caregiver Support Program (NFCSP)
startHere.why: If you're burning out from caregiving, this gets you up to 6 hours weekly of professional respite care.

startHere.name: Tennessee Senior Law Alliance (TSLA)
startHere.why: Free legal help with no income limits covers the most common senior issues — wills, benefits disputes, and elder abuse.

byNeed.description: Daily meal delivery for homebound seniors 60+.
Weekly respite care, training, and support groups for family caregivers 55+.

programs: Tennessee State Health Insurance Assistance Program (TN SHIP) and Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP)
byNeed.description: Free one-on-one Medicare guidance, appeals help, and fraud protection.
Free legal assistance and nursing home advocacy for seniors.
Dedicated staff help seniors access food, housing, and safety services when they fall through the cracks.

quickFacts: 3 programs provide direct benefits or services that require qualification
3 programs offer free resources available to all Tennessee seniors with no income limits
Most benefit programs start with age 60+ for seniors or 55+ for family caregivers
Tennessee has dedicated ombudsman and advocate programs specifically for seniors who need help navigating the system
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Benefits like Tennessee Meals on Wheels and NFCSP respite care require meeting age and need requirements — your parent must be homebound for meals, or you must be 55+ and actively caregiving for respite. Resources like Tennessee Senior Law Alliance and the Long-Term Care Ombudsman are free for any Tennessee senior 60+ regardless of income, assets, or other circumstances.
